steps.title: Set up the quadratic solution form
steps.content:
The answer choices are written as exact radical expressions for the solutions, so solve the quadratic and then match the simplified form. For $x^2 - 6x - 4 = 0$, the coefficients are $a = 1$, $b = -6$, and $c = -4$. First compute the discriminant:
$$b^2 - 4ac = (-6)^2 - 4(1)(-4) = 36 + 16 = 52$$
Then substitute into the quadratic formula:
$$x = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a} = \frac{6 \pm \sqrt{52}}{2}$$

steps.title: Simplify the radical and extract k
steps.content:
Simplify $\sqrt{52} = \sqrt{4 \cdot 13} = 2\sqrt{13}$. Substitute back to get:
$$x = \frac{6 \pm 2\sqrt{13}}{2} = \frac{2(3 \pm \sqrt{13})}{2} = 3 \pm \sqrt{13}$$
One solution is $x = 3 + \sqrt{13}$. Comparing to the form $3 + \sqrt{k}$, we see that $k = 13$, so the correct answer is choice B.
